entry.title: SAP for Me – Adoption Report – Drive More Business Value Realization
entry.content:
This blog is a continuation of my previous blog, Drive more Business Value out of your SAP Public Cloud Solution Subscriptions.

However, in this blog, I want to focus on the Adoption Report included in SAP for Me – Customer Insights as a quick way to understand opportunities for Business Value Realization.

But first, I would like to refer you to the blog SAP Adoption PDF Report generator in Customer Insights (in SAP for Me), written by my SAP colleague Jose Seren, to understand the overall report structure.

Now, similar to my previous blog, if you are an SAP Public Cloud customer running solutions like SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, SAP SuccessFactors, SAP Ariba, etc., or you are a new SAP Business Suite customer running public cloud packages, this report can help you to understand areas of unrealized business value in your integrated system landscape.

While the report is very comprehensive, I only would like to focus on the Business Value Realization section, because it is here where you can see quick wins on value realization by focusing on value drivers for which you currently have no solution capabilities in use or low usage.

Let's look at the following example.

Business Value Realization Overview by Solution Area

In this example, this SAP customer is running several public cloud solutions including SAP Business Data Cloud, SAP SuccessFactors (Core HR and Payroll, Learning and Talent), SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, several SAP Procurement solutions (Ariba Buying and Invoicing, Ariba Sourcing, Ariba Central Invoice Management, etc.), and some SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition add-ons like Cash & Treasury, Multi-Bank Connectivity, and Group Reporting.

Now, the graphic below shows the total number of value drivers that have solution capabilities in use per solution area. For instance, if I look at SAP Business Data Cloud, the report indicates that out of 10 value drivers our customer is only using solution capabilities that impact 5 value drivers—so this means that there are great opportunities to bring more business value to this solution and impact the other 5 value drivers.

Business Value Realization Overview by Solution Area – Value Drivers with used and unused solution capabilities

Now, if we drill down in the report for SAP Business Data Cloud, the report presents to me the value drivers that currently have unused solution capabilities, which I can analyze to bring more business value to this solution area.

Detailed Business Value Realization – Business Data Cloud - Value Drivers with used and unused solution capabilities

As you can see from the report, I have an opportunity to start using solution capabilities that can impact these five value drivers.

Let me focus on the value driver “Reduce data management cost” in SAP Business Data Cloud—the report itself includes the solution capabilities that I can start activating to bring more business value. You can find them at the end of the Adoption Report under “Unrealized Business Value by Solution Area.”

Unrealized Business Value – Value drivers that can impact business outcomes by activating solution capability not used

Per the figure above, the customer could activate “Analytics Cataloging” solution capability to impact the value driver “reduce data management cost.”  However, you can see that this solution capability will also impact other value drivers, including increase user confidence in data and improve analytics FTE productivity.

Unrealized Business Value – Value drivers that can impact business outcomes by activating solution capability not used

Now, the customer could also activate “Story Extensibility” solution capability to impact the value driver “reduce data management cost.”  However, you can see that this solution capability will also impact other value drivers, including: reduce data integration cost and improve analytics FTE productivity.

The Adoption Report provides a very fast overview of unrealized business value.  Alternatively to the report, and to explore the value drivers, solution capabilities, and opportunities to realize more business value, you can use the Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value section in SAP for Me, as I explained in my previous blog.

SAP for Me - Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value

After completing the analysis of the Business Data Cloud, the customer can move to any other area like SAP SuccessFactors in which three value drivers can be impacted with the activation of new solution capabilities.

Detailed Business Value Realization – SAP SuccessFactors Core HR and Payroll - Value Drivers with used and unused solution capabilities

Or decide to analyze unrealized value on the SAP Ariba Solutions and/or SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. Since these are more comprehensive solutions, there is much more impact on additional value drivers of new solution capabilities not used by the customer.

Detailed Business Value Realization – SAP Ariba Solutions and SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - Value Drivers with used and unused solution capabilities

As indicated before, the customer can also focus on unused value drivers that can impact several solutions in their landscape. For that, the customer can use the Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value section in SAP for Me, as I explained in my previous blog.

SAP for Me - Cloud Functional Usage – Business Value

I hope this Adoption Report included in SAP for Me provides you with some ideas to bring more business outcomes for your entire public cloud landscape.

Keep in mind that solution capabilities are expanding from traditional functional scope to also include SAP Business AI, so SAP will continue to add more in this area.

Remember, this is not the only tool that can be used; SAP has other tools such as SAP Signavio, SAP WalkMe, SAP LeanIX, and SAP Cloud ALM that can be used in tandem to help drive more business value from your SAP subscription. I encourage you to discuss these points with your SAP Customer Success Manager, SAP Account Executive, or SAP Partner. They may be able to provide you with additional recommendations and guidance.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @Marco_Valencia (SAP Business Suite Customer Success Management).

entry.title: SAP Ariba Roadmap: Quality Gates Checklist in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
Imagine you’re halfway through your Ariba implementation, and suddenly you discover that key testing activities from earlier phases weren’t completed. What a roadblock! You start to get anxious and stressed and you are wondering how to solve this problem and still meet the deadlines. If you’ve been involved in a project before, you know that sinking feeling all too well.

This is exactly where Quality Gates come to the rescue. At the end of each phase - Prepare, Explore, Realize, and Deploy - there is a task specifically designed to help you check whether all the key deliverables have been completed, align with stakeholders, validate the activities and configurations executed, and think of mitigation plans if something was missed. Think of it as your project’s safety net, catching issues before they grow into bigger problems.

For those using Road Map Viewer, you will have a ‘Conduct Project Quality Gate’ task and an Excel file as an accelerator with easy questions and answers to help you validate your deliveries. You will have to download it and share it with other stakeholders when needed.

But here’s the big news: if you’re working in SAP Cloud ALM, the Quality Gate Checklist is now available directly within the system. That means you no longer need to download the Excel file. You can answer questions, track the status of each item, add comments, and tag the responsible stakeholders - all in one place. This makes Quality Gates more actionable and ensures everyone on the project team stays aligned. See the video below with an example of how to locate this information and answer the questions.

 

With this improvement, your SAP Ariba projects can start 2026 on the right foot. Quality Gates Checklist in SAP Cloud ALM makes it easier than ever to reduce risks, stay aligned, and keep your project moving smoothly.

Feel free to share your comments, suggestions, or feedback in this blog post or directly in Road Map Viewer. We will be happy to assist and improve the quality of our roadmaps!

entry.title: 2602 Early Release Series for SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network releases
entry.content:
Sign up now for the 2602 Early Release Series

Follow this blog to receive notification of new series as soon as registration opens.

SAP strives to equip our customers and partners with valuable release readiness resources for each SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network quarterly release.

 

Early Release Series

The Early Release Series webcasts cover features planned for the upcoming SAP Ariba and SAP Business Network quarterly release.  This platform, available to customers and partners, has a clear mission: deliver an interactive forum to engage with SAP product experts providing simple, direct answers to your most pressing questions.  There is a different session for each product area with content tailored for the specific audience:

  • Supplier Management & Risk
  • Procure to Pay
  • Source to Contract
  • Business Network

If you want to learn more and actively engage with SAP subject matter experts, we encourage you to participate in these informative sessions.  Register now!

Quarterly Release Communications

Notice of upcoming quarterly releases will be communicated to Buyer customers three weeks prior to the release date.  Suppliers receive notice two weeks prior.  Releases are communicated via email notification (look for subject line starting with: Advance Release Notice).

Anyone from your organization that has chosen to subscribe to product notifications will receive these announcements. 

The release calendar for SAP Business Network  and release calendar SAP Ariba Applications identify release timing several quarters in advance.

Where to find out more details?

On demand replays of webcasts from completed quarterly releases can be consumed from Release Readiness Resources: Webcast Archive Page

entry.title: SAP Ariba Procure to Order 2602 Release Key Innovations
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Community, and welcome to our 2602 Key Innovations blog.
In this post, we’re sharing some key updates included in the Q1 2026 release in the procure to order space.

The focus this release covers flexibility in the Guided Buying RFQ process as well as ability to import contracts into SAP Ariba guided buying to help drive greater overall contract compliance and in turn more realised savings.

 

Use Standard Shipping Address for Request for Quotes in Guided Buying (GUIDEB-1267)

Based on the type of item and category, your organization may allow you to request a quote from one or more suppliers as the logical, stramlined process.

With this feature we have introduced more flexibility to select the ship-to address in the RFQ process in guided buying, you can now configure guided buying to either:

Display the user's default ship-to address on RFQ’s generated through guided buying (this is the default option)

Or

Allow users to choose an alternate shipping address for RFQ’s if better aligned to your companies processes

In order to do this a new parameter needs to be enabled by your system administrator: (PARAM_ENABLE_RFQ_SHIP_TO_ADDRESS_IN_USER_CHOSEN_ENTITY)

 

Import Purchase Contracts into SAP Ariba Buying Solutions  (ACC-267)

This feature enables buyers to import purchase contracts from SAP S/4HANA into SAP Ariba buying solutions as a customer configured option using the corresponding parameter ‘Automatic approval of contract requests sent using web services’

In SAP Ariba buying solutions, you can publish non-catalog items in the contract compliance module, which generates subscriptions for those items to make them available in the catalog search to help drive contract compliance.

The import utilizes web services capability in SAP Integration Suite, Managed Gateway for Spend Management and SAP Business Network, which connects SAP S/4HANA and SAP Ariba buying solutions.

For more related technical information you can also refer to:

Integration Support to Import Purchase Contracts from SAP ERP to SAP Ariba Buying Solutions via Web Services

Automatic approval of contract requests sent using web services.

 

Enable Users to Join the SAP User Research Panel (GUIDEB-1259)

With this feature, respondents of the user experience survey in guided buying can provide additional feedback by joining the SAP User Research Panel.

This allows a continuous feedback mechanism where it’s needed, in the application.

This in turn will help SAP product teams to collect continuous feedback and further focus the innovation Roadmap where its important.

This feature introduces changes to the user experience survey. Now, after users complete the survey, a thank you message and an option to join the SAP User Research Panel displays.

 

We hope these features will provide a blend of enhancements that will help to streamline process, increase adoption and add valuable feedback channels into SAP Product. Thanks for reading.

 

Resources:

  • Feature related materials:
    • SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)
    • Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)
  • SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page
  • SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network
  • What’s New

entry.title: SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management 2602 Release Key Innovations
entry.content:
1.  Launch Automatic Matching Manually (CIM-2465)

You can now trigger automatic matching directly from the Manage Supplier Invoices Centrally app while editing a draft invoice. With the new "Match Automatically" button, you can quickly validate invoice data against purchase orders or receipts without leaving your workflow.

Once you have chosen Match Automatically, you can determine which line items will be considered when you launch the automatic matching:

  • All items
  • Non-manually assigned items
  • Unassigned items
  • Items with errors

Any existing posting data that is provided for the selected line items will be deleted and recreated through the automatic matching. The supplier invoice will be saved and move to the process step Reference Object Matching.

Example of the User Interface

The screenshot below shows a new Match Automatically button along with the options for automatic matching in the Manage:

Benefits:

  • Saves time: Eliminate the need to navigate between screens to initiate matching.
  • Improves accuracy: Reduce errors by automating the validation process.
  • Boosts efficiency: Speed up invoice approvals with fewer manual steps.

This enhancement helps you to process invoices faster while maintaining compliance and control.

2.  Decision Tables in Configure Assignment Group Rules App (CIM-2861)

Managing invoice assignments just got smarter. With this release, the Configure Assignment Group Rules app now supports Decision Tables, making it easier to define and apply complex routing rules.

When creating a variant in the Configure Assignment Group Rules app, you can now select Decision Table in the Assignment Group/User Rule field within the Step Configuration tab. Below are the available input parameter combinations for different decision tables. Assignment groups and users are the output:

  • Company Code and WBS Element
  • Company Code and Cost Center
  • Company Code, Invoicing Party, and Gross Amount
  • Company Code and G/L Account
  • Commodity Code 

Example of the User Interface

The figure below shows the list of decision tables available in the Configure Decision Tables app for the assignment group and user determination:

Why it matters:

  • Simplified rule configuration:  Decision tables provide a clear, structured way to define assignment rules, reducing manual effort and minimizing errors.
  • Enhanced flexibility:  Use decision tables directly in the Assignment Group/User Rule field under the Step Configuration tab when setting up variants.
  • Improved accuracy:  Ensure invoices are routed to the right teams or users based on predefined conditions, improving processing speed and compliance.

This feature empowers your finance and procurement teams to handle exceptions and approvals more efficiently, reducing bottlenecks and accelerating invoice processing.

3.  Assignment Group/User determined by Custom Logic in the Configure Assignment Group Rules app (CIM-1759)

With this release, it is possible to determine the assignment group or user, or a combination of both, using custom logic.

In the Configure Assignment Group Rules app, you now have the option to choose an assignment group/user rule. This is available in the new Assignment Group/User Rule field in the Step Configuration tab.

If you choose Assignment Group/User Determined by Custom Logic in this field, it allows you to determine the assignment group or user of a step without specifying them during variant creation. The determination is done outside the app via an API.

Example of the User Interface

The screenshot below shows the Step Configuration tab in the Configure Assignment Group Rules app:

4.  Simulation of Workflow Steps before Submission of Invoice (CIM-1759)

The latest release of SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management introduces exciting new features designed to simplify workflows, reduce errors, and improve collaboration between finance teams and suppliers. 

Key Benefits of this Feature:

  • Enhanced Transparency – See the full approval path upfront, so there are no surprises.
  • Proactive Issue Resolution – Spot potential delays or missing approvals before submission.
  • Improved Efficiency – Reduce back-and-forth by ensuring invoices follow the correct workflow from the start.

Changes to the User Interface

The screenshot below shows tab when there is no request for information available and an approval workflow is yet to start:

The next screenshot shows the tab when a request for information has started but an approval workflow is yet to start:

 The final screenshot below shows the tab when all the workflows have started:

This enhancement is just one way we’re making invoice management smarter and more intuitive for our customers.

5.  New Field for Invoice Management (CIM-3034)

A new Contact Person field has been added to the invoice detail page, allowing users to designate a responsible individual for managing and approving invoices. This feature enhances accountability and streamlines the invoice approval process, ensuring timely payments and efficient workflow management.

This field is hidden by default. You can control its visibility using the Configure Business Data Scope app. For more information, see About Field Control. You can use the value help to enter a name in this field.

You can also set the contact person as an approver of an invoice. To do this, select Contact Person for The Invoice in the Agent Determination Rule field of the Step Configuration tab.  This applies when creating a variant or adding a step in an existing variant in the Manage Workflows for Supplier Invoice Approval app.

In addition, you can configure automatic allocation of a supplier invoice to the name entered. To achieve this, select Contact Person for the Invoice in the Assignment Group/User Rule field of the Step Configuration tab. This applies when creating a variant or adding a step in an existing variant in the Configure Assignment Group Rules app.  

A new Contact Person filter is also added to the invoice list page to filter the invoices based on it. This filter and the column are not available by default. See Adapting Filters and Creating Variants for more information on how to show or hide the filters and the columns.  Any update made in this new field is recorded in the activity log.

Changes to the User Interface

The screenshot below shows you the Contact Person field:

Benefits:

  • Enhanced accountability – Designate a specific contact for each invoice, reducing confusion and delays.
  • Streamlined approvals – Faster processing with direct responsibility, leading to timely payments.
  • Improved workflow efficiency – Better tracking and communication for smoother invoice management.

This small but impactful feature helps teams work more effectively, reducing bottlenecks and improving collaboration across finance and procurement teams.

What’s Next?

Stay tuned for more innovations in SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management as we continue to enhance automation, visibility, and control in your invoice management processes.

Have questions or want to learn more? Reach out to your SAP representative or explore our help documentation at http://help.sap.com.

Let’s make invoice management simpler, smarter, and faster—together.

Resources:

  • Feature related materials:
    • SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)
    • Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)
  • SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page
  • SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network
  • What’s New

entry.title: What's New in 2602, Release Navigator for Spend Management
entry.content:
Welcome to Q1 2026! A new year brings fresh innovations, and we’re excited to introduce the 2602 updates for the Release Navigator for Spend Management. This update is all about enhancing content maintenance, helping you stay ahead of the release!

Tool Overview

The Release Navigator for Spend Management is a tool provided by SAP Enterprise Support designed to be your central hub for understanding and managing new software releases for SAP Ariba, SAP Business Network, SAP Fieldglass, & SAP Concur. It allows you to easily locate essential resources for upcoming releases and quickly find cross-solution materials, documentation, webcasts, and more all in one tool!

  

New & Updated Content:

  • 2602 What's New Viewer
  • 2602 Early Release Webcasts
  • 2602 Community Blogs:
    • SAP Supplier Management & Risk
    • SAP Procure-to-Order
    • SAP Source-to-Contract
    • SAP Category Management
  • 2602 Feature Spreadsheet

2602 Feature Highlights:

SAP Ariba Procurement:

  • Generating Contract Summaries in SAP Ariba Contracts using AI [SCONTR-1266]
  • Modifying Event Duration and Searching Sourcing Requests Using Joule [SOURCE-2534]
  • Additional AI Copilot Joule Capabilities for Finding Suppliers, Supplier Contacts, and Supplier Management Projects [SLP-2411]

SAP Business Network:

  • Mass Aprove Order Confirmation Deviations [CSC-69956]
  • Sending Additional Address Details from TM to BN4L [FC-282]
  • Enhancement to Buyer Groups User Interface [NP-69370]

Innovation Schedule

To ensure a smooth user experience, the Release Navigator introduces new user‑experience updates once per year in August, alongside refreshed release content.

The Release Navigator will update automatically over the coming weeks—no action is required on your end.

Disclaimer: These dates are informational only and may change. The Readiness@Scale team has final decisions over any UX modifications.

Connect with Us

Your feedback helps shape future enhancements to the Release Navigator. We invite you to:

  • Complete our feedback survey
  • Share ideas or requests in the comments section
  • Learn more about the Readiness@Scale team
  • Explore additional content

Thank you for your continued engagement and support.

Best Run,
Readiness@Scale

SAP Enterprise Support

entry.title: Enable complex service Procurement in Ariba with service entry sheet export to SAP
entry.content:
Background:

Service Procurement refers to the process of acquiring services from external suppliers to meet the needs of an organization. Unlike products, which invoices purchasing physical products, service procurement focuses on obtaining intangible offerings that can range from consulting and legal services to cleaning and IT support. 

SAP Ariba procurement solutions provide businesses with a powerful suite of tools designed to address the complexities associated with service procurement.

Purpose: 

This functionality will enable users to create service sheets for purchase orders with the item category "Service" in Ariba Buying. Upon completion of the approval flow, the service sheet will be transferred to the S/4 system for validation and creation against the purchase order. Once the creation response is received from S/4 to Ariba through the CIG system, Ariba will then communicate the service sheet creation to the supplier through the SAP Business Network.

Configuration Steps in Ariba B&I

Step 1:   Request your DSC to submit a case to support asking for this feature to be enabled. 

  1. Create Service entry Sheet
  2. ExportServiceSheet
  3. Toggle SINV-5399 - Edit and resubmit the SES action for service sheet failed in external system due to ERP push error

Step 2:  Basic self-service parameters to be enabled in Intelligent configuration Manager

 

ParameterValueDescription
Application. Procure.EnableServiceitemFieldinRequsition YesAllows users to mark requisition line items as service line items
Application.Procure.EnableitemCategoryOnRequsitionLine YesSpecifies whether the item category option to mark line items as service items is displayed on the requisiton summary page
Application.ServiceSheet.EnableERPIntegrationYesSpecifies whether your site sends service sheets to the SAP ERP for approval. 
 Application.ServiceSheet.ServiceSheet ExternalApprovalEnabled  YesDetermines whether service sheets must undergo approval in SAP ERP before moving to the processes state in the buying solution
Application.ServiceSheet.AllowNonCatalogItem YesSpecifies whether suppliers can add non catalog items to service sheet in Ariba 
Application.ServiceSheet. EnableResendingOfFailedServiceSheetsToSBN YesDetermine whether the SAP Ariba solution initiates the resending of failed service sheets to SAP business network
Application.ServiceSheet.FixSESStatusIntegrationForCIGYesDetermine whether the status must be reset to the previous value for cancelled or force rejected service sheets that fail to export to SAP ERP from the Arina solution. 

Step 3: Enable below managed gateway for spend and Network Integration tasks. 

  1. Export Service Sheet Asynchronously
  2. Export Service Sheet Response to External System Asynchronously
  3. Import Service Sheet Approval Status
  4. Import Service Sheet Response Asynchronously
  5. Import Service Sheet Status Response Asynchronously

** Disable webservice format tasks in integration configuration as they might interfere with the export Behavior. 

Step 4: Add users to the below groups

  1. Service Sheet Entry User
  2. Service Sheet Editor 

Configuration Steps in SAP

Step 5: Enable below parameters in S4

SPRO-->SAP reference IMG-->Integration with Other SAP Components--> Managed Gateway for Spend&Network for Buyer-->SAP Business Network Integration-->Application Specific Settings -->Service Sheet-->Maintain Parameters for Service Sheet

Parameter in S4MandatoryDescription
ENTRYSHEET_CREATENoIf the purchase order at SAP ERP is in the edit mode, the system would not allow to create a service entry sheet. Configure the duration (in seconds) for which the system would attempt to create a service entry when the purchase order at ERP is in edit mode
ENABLE_SES_ITERATIONNo This configuration is only for SAP Ariba Buying and Invoicing to avoid failure of the transaction document due to a locking issue while processing multiple service sheets for the same purchase order service type item

SPRO-->SAP reference IMG-->Integration with Other SAP Components--> Managed Gateway for Spend&Network for Buyer-->SAP Business Network Integration-->Application Specific Settings -->Service Sheet Response-->Maintain Parameters for Service Sheet Response

 

Parameter in S4MandatoryDescription
SES_RESPONSE_ENABLEDNoEnable or disable the Ariba provided enhancements for service sheet response

Step 6: Defining Output message control for service sheet in S4 System

SPRO-->SAP reference IMG-->Integration with Other SAP Components--> Managed Gateway for Spend&Network for Buyer-->SAP Business Network Integration-->Application Specific Settings -->Service Sheet Response-->Define Message Output Control for Service Sheet Response

Application: ES - Entry Sheet

Program: ARBCIG_ENTRYSHEET_UPDATE

FORM routine: SEND_ENTRYSHEET_RESPONSE

Step 7: Maintaining Event Linkages to Generate Service Sheet Responses

Create and activate the SES_RESPONSE event type linkage to generate service sheet responses after you revoke or delete service sheets

Step 8: BADI implementation related to S4 service entry sheet creation and approval. 

  • For auto approve SES in SAP - Implement BADI ARBCIG_SES_CREATE - Method UPDATE_SES_BAPITABLES - pass Service sheet header acceptance value 
  • To send approve status from S4 to Ariba - Implement BADI ARBCIG_SESR_UPDATE - method POST_CHANGE_SESR_DATA - using call function ARBCIG_SAP_RESPONSE_TO_P2P send header approval status in import pull  ServiceSheetImportPullRequest
  • For Ariba SES to be rejected outside financial posting period - Implement BADI ARBCIG_SES_DELETE - method UPDATE_SES_DELETE. Pass the posting date as SES rejection date (current date) 

Conclusion:

Enabling complex service procurement in Ariba with the export of service entry sheets to SAP streamlines the procurement process and enhances efficient across organizations. This integration not only allows for a more organized method of service procurement but also ensures that the data is accurately captured and processed in SAP.

This end-to-end step configuration guide serves as a comprehensive roadmap for organizations looking to implement and optimize complex service procurement with a focus on practical integration with SAP.

entry.title: Retrieving Users and Groups via SCIM: Integrating SAP Ariba with SAP Cloud Identity Services
entry.content:
SAP Ariba SCIM API

The SAP Ariba SCIM API is a REST API based on the SCIM (System for Cross-domain Identity Management) 2.0 specification. It is intended for use as an interface between SAP Ariba cloud solutions and SAP Cloud Identity Services to transfer user information as part of user management.

Prerequisites

To call the methods of this SAP Ariba SCIM API you must have SAP Ariba Procurement or Strategic Sourcing Realms.

ACCESS SAP Ariba APIs

1. Access SAP Ariba APIs : https://developer.ariba.com/api/home

2. Select the right Data Center where your SAP Ariba Realms located, in my case is Japan Data Center:

3. To find the API Specs, click Discover, fill the right solution, Procurement or Strategic Sourcing, search the page with SCIM API for User and Group Master Data and click.

4. Now we will request SCIM API. Go to Manage, click + to Create a New Application.

5. Fill necessary information and click submit:

6. Application created, you can click Action and then click Request API Access, then select the SCIM API and select SAP Ariba Realms that you want to retrieve data from. Then click Submit.

7. Screen shows as below and auto-Approval will happen in about 1 day:

 

8. Once approved, click Actions and click Generate Auth Secret. Note down them and will be used in API.

CALL SAP Ariba SCIM API

1. My personal preference is to test post and get in PowerShell and once it works ok, configure in Postman or Bruno to have a more filtered or formatted view of the result. Now open PowerShell:

2. Type below into powershell to post and get the Access_Token:

3. Enter and you will find the necessary access token and token type as below, note them down:

4. Now use the access token above to type below command, enter and all users will be listed as below:

Now all necessary info to call SCIM and retrieve user and group data is ready. 

CALL SAP Ariba SCIM API in Bruno

1. Change to Bruno UI, create a new request, select Type as HTTP , fill Request Name , select URL  with POST and fill the URL, then click Create.

2. Go to Headers, configure Content-Type as application/x-www-form-urlencoded.

3. Then go to params, configure below 3 attributes:

grant_type : client_credentials

client_id : Oauth Client Id

client_secret : Oauth Secret Generated

4. Run the request and note down the access_token:

 

5. Create a new request, select Type as HTTP , fill Request Name , select URL  with GET and URL, then click Create. 

6. Go to Headers, fill below four attributes:

Authorization : <token_type> <access_token>

apiKey : Application API Key

x-anId : Realm ANID *for test with -T

Accept : application/scim+json

7. Run the request, you can see more formatted data as below:

8. Based on the specs below, you can search more data with filters etc.

For example, you want to get User with email address equals below:

9. You can also retrieve all group data as below:

 

Identity Directory API (CIS - IdDS)

Manage users, groups and custom schemas in the cloud. Help Portal : https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/identity-directory-scim-rest-api

Prerequisites

To call the methods of this SCIM REST API you must have a system as administrator with an assigned Manage Users role. 

API SPECS

Please find detailed API info from here : https://api.sap.com/api/IdDS_SCIM/overview 

 

ACCESS Identity Directory API:

Make sure you are Administrator User in CIS - idDS:

1. Administrator can login to CIS via link : https://<Tenant ID>.accounts.ondemand.com/admin/

2. To check other Administrators, click and select as below:

 

3. Call API via PowerShell by typing as below, click enter and you will see all users.

-u UserID:Password

-H "Accept: application/scim+json" `

-H "Content-Type: application/scim+json"

4. Now use Bruno to get better formatted result. Create a Request as below:

5. Clich Auth, then select Basic Auth from dropdown list, Fill UserName and password with your Administrator(User) email address and password.

6. Click Headers, configure Accept and Content-Type as application/scim+json.

7. Execute and all users of CIS(IdDS) retrieved as below:

8. Add filter based on user Login Name, execute then you can see the user has been extracted:

9. You can also retrieve group data by filter as below:

 

 

Summary

This blog walked through how to retrieve user and group master data using SCIM 2.0 APIs from both SAP Ariba and SAP Cloud Identity Services (Identity Directory / IdDS)—a foundational requirement for user provisioning and synchronization scenarios.

Instead of introducing the full functionality of Identity Provisioning Service (IPS), the focus was placed on the practical API calls that IPS relies on internally: how to access the API specifications, request API access, authenticate, and retrieve users and groups using SCIM-compliant endpoints. Both PowerShell and Bruno were used to demonstrate how to call these APIs and inspect responses in a structured and readable way.

By understanding how SAP Ariba and Identity Directory expose identity data through SCIM—along with supported filters, attributes, and authentication mechanisms—you gain deeper insight into IPS properties, mappings, and transformations. This knowledge is especially critical when enabling SAP Ariba Joule, where accurate user and group synchronization across SAP Ariba, SAP Cloud Identity Services, and SAP Build Work Zone is essential.

In short, mastering these SCIM APIs is not just about calling endpoints—it is about building a solid foundation for reliable identity integration in SAP’s AI-driven and Business AI–focused landscape.

entry.title: AI Readiness: You don’t need an orchestra yet, you need a steady bass line
entry.content:
Any other big music fans in the procurement world out there? I know I am. And while I like music from most genres, what I really love is some intricate symphonic soul, like Isaac Hayes, or Curtis Mayfield. Strings, horns, keys, even a vibraphone. I get caught up in all the different sounds, the complex chords, how it all comes together.  But a real groove needs a great bass line. If that one thing is off, the whole production feels wrong.

That’s how a lot of my customer conversations on AI in procurement feel lately. Everyone’s focusing on those extras – AI assistants, instant answers and insights, getting to talk to AI like a person, and shiny demos of the vast possibilities with SAP Joule. And while those things DO matter, and they are the end goal, without that steady bassline carrying us through, those other sounds won’t land the way they’re supposed to.


So what’s the bass line in SAP AI?

In SAP landscapes, a solid foundation comes down to a few important things.

  • Clean, consistent identity and access (IAS)
  • Reliable integrations and flows of data (BTP)
  • And, incredibly important, clear ownership and usable data in systems like Ariba

When identity is fragmented, data between systems becomes inconsistent, or processes start to vary depending on who’s involved and who’s taking action. When this happens, AI doesn’t become on-beat and insightful, it becomes…improvisational, like jazz (but not in the good way, more the kind that just feels like chaos). We need to feel the rhythm first, since procurement is often where we see issues show up first.

Maybe your suppliers don’t line up across systems. Or risk is managed in a separate system, and doesn’t connect to anything today. And, likely the most common thing we hear, users work around the process because it’s faster than doing it the right way.

These things aren’t always a technology problem, but more of a rhythm problem.  Which is why building the foundation (bass line) is so important.

How are others laying down the groove and actually making progress? A few ways:

  1. Standardizing across their SAP applications:  This doesn’t mean everything has to be identical or locked down overnight. It usually starts much more simply: agreeing on how users authenticate, how suppliers are identified, and which system is the source of truth for what. When different SAP applications each have their own rules, logins, and data assumptions, things get out of sync fast. Standardization is what keeps the rhythm steady across the landscape instead of constantly drifting off-beat.

  2. Building strong integrations through BTP:  BTP is often talked about in very abstract terms, but in practice, this is about making sure data can move cleanly and predictably between systems. For procurement, this mostly means having supplier data flow consistently between Ariba and your ERP, risk info actually connecting back to suppliers, and integrations that are monitored and understood. When those integrations are brittle or one-off, that means AI is going to make guesses. We don’t want that.

  3. Cleaning up supplier lifecycles and ownership in Ariba:  Who owns onboarding? Who makes updates to supplier data? If you’re thinking, “It depends,” then that’s where problems likely start. Clarifying ownership of supplier lifecycles in Ariba doesn’t just improve data quality, rather it makes downstream automation and AI insights far more reliable.

  4. Making processes easy enough that users can follow them:  If a process is technically correct but painful in practice, users will work around it. Simplifying steps, reducing exceptions, and designing for how people actually work is part of the composition.

  5. Policies in place that are actually enforced, both internally and with suppliers:  Policies only help if they show up in the systems people use every day. When rules are enforced inconsistently (or not at all), data quality erodes, as does trust in the system.

That’s it, that’s the groove. Once you find this rhythm, the rest of the instruments can shine, take solos, and please a crowd. With that foundation, AI features become easier to adopt, and give better insights, and automation helps to reduce work, improve cycle time, ensure compliance, and other things you care about, instead of just confusing the people who actually have to use it.

entry.title: Balancing Conflicting Priorities of Procurement and Supply Chain
entry.content:
From Factory Floor to Forecast: What Matt Donegan’s Procurement Stories Reveal About Where SAP Customers Are Heading 🚀

If you’ve ever wondered why procurement debates can get so heated—especially in manufacturing—Matt Donegan’s podcast conversation is a masterclass in the real-world tension behind “simple” sourcing decisions. 🧩 Beneath the lighthearted dog-and-TikTok opener, Matt digs into an issue SAP customers and partners know well: direct materials procurement isn’t just about savings—it’s about keeping production running, protecting quality, and managing risk across a supply chain that’s often far less predictable than it looks on a dashboard. ⚙️

What makes Matt’s perspective especially valuable is that it’s grounded in plant-site reality. He’s lived the pressure of missing ingredients, uncertain shipments, and the ripple effect that one supplier decision can create across operations. And for organizations modernizing with SAP, his stories point to a clear message: the future belongs to teams that connect sourcing strategy with shop-floor execution—using data, collaboration, and technology that people actually adopt. 📈

Direct vs. Indirect: Why the “Lifeblood” Spend Gets Different Rules 🧠

One of the most important framing points in the discussion is the distinction between direct materials, indirect materials, and services—because the business consequences are radically different. 🏭

  • Direct materials are inputs that become part of the finished product. They’re planned against forecasts, tied to production schedules, and directly impact what customers receive on a daily basis. ✅
  • Indirect materials support the business (think facility supplies, office goods, and warehouse items), but don’t appear on the bill of materials. 🧻
  • Services keep operations running—from maintenance to repairs to cleaning and MRO-related work. 🛠️

Why does this matter to SAP customers and partners? Because direct procurement runs on a different clock and carries different risk. A late shipment of a key ingredient can halt a production line—turning a “good deal” into an expensive disruption. ⏱️

Plant Managers Aren’t Graded on Savings—They’re Graded on Output 🎯

Matt’s biggest insight is also one of the most practical: plant leaders are running a business inside the business. 📦 They manage inputs, transform them, and ship outputs—and their priority is being a reliable supplier to their customers.

From that lens, “success” looks like:

  • First-pass quality (materials meet spec without rework) ✅
  • On-time, in-full delivery (OTIF) (materials arrive when and how much is needed) 📅
  • Available-to-promise reliability (customers can trust commitments) 🤝

This is where procurement gets tricky: centralized sourcing teams may be measured primarily on year-over-year savings, while operations teams are measured on uptime and performance. 📊 The result is a familiar tug-of-war: cost optimization versus production certainty.

For SAP ecosystems, this is a reminder that transformation isn’t just system change—it’s KPI alignment and governance design. 🧭

The Real Pain Point: Global Supply Chains Still Have “Visibility Gaps” 🌍

One of Matt’s most vivid stories is about overseas procurement: sending a PO, getting a quick confirmation, and then enduring weeks of uncertainty. 🚢

Even when suppliers are reliable, international logistics introduces unpredictable variables:

  • port delays
  • customs holds
  • limited tracking precision
  • last-mile scheduling issues
  • constraints on receiving capacity at the plant 🧱

Matt highlights a dimension many teams underestimate: the human and physical reality of receiving. Materials don’t just “arrive.” Someone must unload, inspect, store safely (sometimes in specialized containers), and route it into production. 👷

For SAP customers, this underscores why visibility isn’t just a “nice-to-have.” It is operational resilience. And for partners, it reinforces the value of designs that connect procurement, logistics, inventory, and quality processes into one continuous picture. 🧩

Consignment: The Underused Strategy That Solves Two Problems at Once 💡

One of the most actionable tactics discussed is consignment inventory, which Matt calls a best friend—and for good reason. 🤝

In a consignment model:

  • the supplier ships material to the buyer’s site
  • the buyer does not pay until the material is pulled for use
  • inventory can sit nearby as a buffer without burdening working capital 💰

This creates two strategic advantages:

  1. Operational safety: the plant has backup stock when overseas shipments slip. 🛡️
  2. Working capital relief: millions in inventory can be available without sitting on the buyer’s balance sheet. 📉

The catch? Consignment depends on strong supplier trust—typically built through years of partnership and reliable consumption patterns. 🧱 This is an important point for procurement leaders: resilience often comes from relationship maturity, not just contract terms.

The Collaboration Playbook: Data, Shared Strategy, and Stakeholder Buy-In 📊

When asked how organizations can reduce friction between central sourcing and operations, Matt returns to a simple idea: be data-driven—and bring stakeholders along early. 🧾

He describes an internal “negotiation” process between sourcing and plant teams:

  • which suppliers are non-negotiable due to quality risk
  • where contracts can be rebalanced without harming performance
  • how allocation (local vs. overseas) can hedge risk over time ⚖️

The critical enablers here are:

  • shared metrics (not just siloed KPIs) 📐
  • supplier performance data (quality, OTIF, rework rates) 🔍
  • tools people actually use (adoption matters as much as capability) ✅

For SAP customers, this is a blueprint: procurement transformation succeeds when the system supports cross-functional decision-making—not when it simply digitizes old silos. 🏗️

Future-Facing Take: AI Will Elevate the Human Side of Procurement 🤖➡️🧑‍🤝‍🧑

Matt’s forward-looking statement is refreshingly balanced: yes, automation will expand—especially across tactical work and data-heavy tasks. 🤖 But his “hot take” is that what will differentiate procurement professionals is not purely technical mastery.

Instead, the standouts will be those who excel at:

  • Stakeholder influence internally 🗣️
  • Strategic supplier relationships externally 🤝
  • Joint innovation with partners 💡
  • Risk mitigation through trust, audits, and shared plans 🛡️

In other words: as AI absorbs more of the “math,” procurement becomes even more human. And that’s a powerful lens for SAP customers investing in intelligent procurement: the goal isn’t replacing people—it’s freeing them to do higher-value work. 🚀

Conclusion: The Next Procurement Advantage Is Integration—Of Data and People ✅

Matt Donegan’s stories remind us that procurement isn’t a back-office function in manufacturing—it’s a lever that can protect uptime, improve quality, and reduce volatility across the supply chain. 🏭

For SAP customers and partners, the implications are clear:

  • Align KPIs across sourcing and operations to reduce internal conflict 🎯
  • Use data to shift debates from opinion to evidence 📊
  • Build supplier partnerships that unlock resilience tools like consignment 🤝
  • Invest in technology that supports collaboration—not just compliance 🧩
  • Prepare for AI-enabled procurement where relationship skills matter more than ever 🤖✨

If there’s one takeaway worth reflecting on, it’s this: the best procurement strategies don’t optimize a number—they optimize the business. 📈

https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/episode-027-aligning-procurement-and-supply-chain/id1764078646?i=1000749416873

https://open.spotify.com/show/5twXP1NehSqgCAznTnzQ5H

entry.title: Troubleshooting Ariba-Joule Integration errors
entry.content:
Introduction:🚀 Cracking the SAP Ariba–Joule Integration common errors:

Over the past few months, I’ve seen a common pattern across multiple customer implementations: brilliant landscapes struggling with Navigation Flow glitches, incomplete Transactional prompts, or inconsistent User Sync—all caused by a handful of tiny but critical configuration gaps across Ariba, IAS, IPS, and Build Work Zone.

To help accelerate troubleshooting and avoid post‑go‑live surprises, I’ve put together a practical breakdown of the most recurring issues and the exact fixes that have consistently stabilized the integration.

If you're enabling Joule for Ariba or supporting customers on their journey, this article will save you hours of guesswork. 🔧💡

Common issues with Navigation flow and resolution: 

Scenario 1:Navigational prompts intermittently return data

Sample prompts: Go my approvals, my task, create engagement request, created sourcing project, open supplier profile etc   

Cause: This could happen, if user has been assigned more than 150 groups in Ariba and those groups are being replicated to IAS and then to Build work zone.  To verify if the issue is due to roles replicated to BWZ,  login to BWZ and user will get error “HTTP ERROR 431”. 

Resolution: Replicate only those standard groups to BWZ which are required for Ariba-Joule integration. You can achieve this by adding a filter parameter ariba.applications.group.filter in the IPS source system (the source system configured to fetch users and groups from Ariba https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/joule-integration-with-sap-ariba-setup-guide/ba-p/14240003 ). Using this filter ensures that only the required groups—and users assigned to those groups—are synchronized from Ariba to IAS and subsequently to BWZ.

Sample value: (displayName eq "Sourcing Agent" or displayName eq "Sourcing Approver")

Important Notes

  • Navigational flow works only with standard groups.
  • If customers assign only custom roles to users in Ariba, they must still replicate the required standard groups from Ariba → IAS → BWZ.
  • Custom groups are not supported, SAP is considering for future release 
  • This filtering process does not impact customer‑specific role assignments:
    • Customers may continue using their custom groups assignment in Ariba as usual
    • Since custom groups map back to standard roles, any Joule‑relevant standard roles tied to custom roles will still function properly through this filtered sync.
  • Recommendation:
    • Maintain a list of standard groups required for Joule navigation.
    • Additionally replicate customer‑specific custom groups only to ensure that all Ariba users sync correctly to CIS

Steps to maintain the parameter:

  • Login to CIS and navigate to identity provision
  • Source system à look for the source system which is created to fetch the user/groups from Ariba
  • Edit and maintain the parameter and run the IPS job as described previously
List of standard roles which are required for Ariba joule Navigational scenario:

Supplier Registration Manager

Supplier Manager

Supplier Qualification Manager

Supplier Risk ESG User

Purchasing Administrator

Supplier Risk Engagement Requestor

Financial Processing Analyst

Supplier Risk Evaluation Expert

AuditBuyer

Supplier Risk Provider Registration Expert

Purchasing Agent

Joule Supplier Profile User

Invoice Manager

Sourcing Project Administrator

Purchasing Manager

Sourcing Agent

Report Manager

Limited Event Administrator

Receiving Manager

Junior Sourcing Agent

Manual Order Processor

SM Questionnaire Manager

Tax Manager

Supplier Risk Engagement Governance Analyst

Purchasing User

Supplier Qualification Request Manager

Executive Approver

Supplier Risk User

Sourcing Approver

Supplier Risk Report Expert

Category Buyer

Supplier Risk Engagement Expert

Supplier Risk Engagement Analyst

Sourcing Manager

Operational Junior Sourcing Agent

Supplier Risk Manager

Supplier Risk Configuration Expert

Supplier Request Manager

SM Process Manager

Event Administrator

Scenario 2:  Error “The application cannot be opened”

Resolution: Create a destination under the sub-account which has been created for joule. Make sure destination name is “NavigationService” follow the below article for more details.

https://help.sap.com/docs/joule/integrating-joule-with-sap/configure-destination-to-use-navigation-service?locale=en-US&q=+NavigationService

Steps:

  • Login to Joule Sub-account
  • Click connectivity à Destination
  • Click create

Scenario 3: Error: You need different permissions to perform this action or I am having trouble processing your request.

Cause : This happen when user doesn’t have the required access in Ariba or if they have the required access, joule related standard roles are not assigned to user in BWZ after replication.  

To verify the roles in BWZ, login to BWZ

  • Check User Role Assignments: In the settings à Identity Provisioning
  • Within "Explore Role Assignments",enter the user ID experiencing the issue
  • Review the list of roles and permissions assigned to the user

Resolution: As described in scenario 1 navigation flow, maintain the joule roles in source system parameter run the job. This will synchronize the user and groups between ariba , IAS and BWZ. Make sure roles are assigned to users in ariba as well. Login to BWZ and verify the roles once again:

Common issues with Transactional flow and resolution:

Scenario 1:When user enters any transactional prompt in joule, user gets results as an informational data.

Sample prompts: List of recent purchases

Cause: This happens when ibn_target not found at the backend. In simple words, the formation synchronization did not happen between the include system despite the fact status of the formation is ready.  

Resolution: Login to global account, exclude and include Ariba buyer and sourcing system within the formations.

 

Scenario 2: When user enters a transactional prompt, there is no response

Cause: This happens when there is no dependencies added to the das-ias applications in CIS

Resolution:

  • Login to IAS
  • Navigate to applications and search for “das-ias” and look for the entry which is there for the sub-account which is created for joule
  • Navigate to dependency section, make sure to maintain APIs for Procurement and Sourcing applications

Delayed User synchronization between Ariba and Cloud identify service: IPS Jobs are getting stuck in running status for few hours  

Steps to run the Job:

  • Login to CIS and navigate to identity provision

Source system à look for the source system which is created to fetch the user/groups from Ariba

 Click on job logs in IPS to check the status:

 Resolution:  By default the value configured for parameter (ariba.applications.group.members.page.size,ariba.applications.user.groups.page.size )  is 50, update it according to your need. If this doesn’t work, log a support ticket with SAP to increase the API rate limit.

Joule Icon Is Missing in Ariba despite completing all the configurations (running boosters, system added successfully in formations, SSO is enabled)

Resolution: Log a ticket with SAP to enable the feature PLUI 2928  at parent and child level

If you are facing any other issues, I would suggest to log a case with support team. To save time, make sure to share the logs and X-Correlation ID with support team. 

How to download the Joule Logs:

  • Login to Ariba application
  • Click on joule icon, reproduce the issue
  • Click on three bars --> settings  --> abouts --> download trances --> include message history --> download 

How to Get X Correlation ID?

  • Login to Ariba system
  • Enable logs:
    • Right-click on browser, select "Inspect"
    • once the Developer Tools are open, locate and click on the "Network" tab
  • Open Joule (keep the Developer Tools open)
  • Reproduce the issue you are experiencing
  • When the issue is reproduced:
    • in the Network tab, identify the most recent API call to Joule. This API call will be named with a UUID (for example: c1234b04-c313-42cd-9be3-a5efe9b0f2d1).
    • click on the API request and go to the "Headers" section;
    • scroll down to find and collect the following fields:
      • X-correlationid
      • Timestamp,
      • JouleDataCenter (you can find this in the "API Request URL")

Ariba-Joule support queues: you may choose the queue based on the nature of the error. 

✨Conclusion
The most common Ariba–Joule issues often stem from small but critical configuration gaps across Ariba, IAS, IPS, and Build Work Zone—and fixing these resolves the majority of navigation, transactional, and sync failures.
With the right configurations in place, Joule delivers the seamless, intelligent experience it’s designed for—helping customers unlock real value without post‑go‑live roadblocks. 🚀

entry.title: From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI Driven Procurement
entry.content:
From Copilot to Colleague: How SAP Is Shaping the Next Era of AI‑Driven Procurement 🤖

Artificial intelligence has crossed a critical threshold in enterprise software. What began as experimentation with chat interfaces has rapidly evolved into a strategic conversation about how work itself is performed. In a recent discussion with Ryan Skorupski, Senior Director of AI/ML at SAP Ariba, the conversation explored how AI is moving from assistive tooling to embedded intelligence—and what that shift means for procurement leaders navigating increasing complexity, data volume, and speed expectations. 🚀

AI Didn’t Arrive Overnight—But the Moment Has Arrived 🌱

While generative AI may feel new, the technologies behind it—machine learning, language modeling, and predictive analytics—have been advancing in academia and research environments for more than a decade. What changed was accessibility. When large language models became available to a broader audience, leaders experienced firsthand how AI could reason across information, synthesize insights, and support decision‑making at scale.

For procurement, this marked a turning point. AI was no longer limited to narrow automation use cases; it became capable of understanding context, language, and intent. As Ryan noted, this moment forced organizations to rethink not just what they automate, but how procurement work should be designed in an AI‑first world. 💡

Beyond Chatbots: The Rise of the Procurement Copilot ✈️

Much of today’s AI adoption centers on copilots—context‑aware assistants embedded directly into enterprise applications. In SAP Ariba, copilots are designed to work alongside users, helping them navigate sourcing events, analyze data, and interact with complex workflows using natural language.

Unlike traditional chatbots, copilots are grounded in business context. They understand procurement data, permissions, and workflows, allowing them to assist with real tasks—not just answer questions. This represents a significant step forward in usability and productivity, particularly for teams balancing speed, compliance, and accuracy. 🔍

The Next Leap: Agentic AI in Procurement 🤝

While copilots enhance productivity, Ryan highlighted that the next major evolution is AI agents—systems capable of taking action, not just providing guidance. An AI agent can assemble an RFP, analyze historical sourcing events, recommend negotiation strategies, and prepare supplier communications with minimal human intervention.

Importantly, the near‑term goal is not perfection or full autonomy. The value today lies in time savings and scale. AI agents can handle large portions of repetitive, rules‑based work, while humans remain in the loop to review, adjust, and approve outcomes. Over time, as trust and accuracy improve, higher levels of autonomy become possible—especially for low‑risk, high‑volume categories. ⚖️

Why Data Foundations Matter More Than Models 🧠

A recurring theme in the discussion was that AI success depends less on algorithms and more on data quality and structure. Procurement data is often fragmented, inconsistently labeled, or repurposed in ways AI cannot easily interpret. AI cannot “clean” data without understanding its meaning.

This is where SAP’s investment in knowledge graphs becomes critical. By layering semantic meaning over enterprise data—defining what each field, object, and API represents in natural language—AI systems can reason about business data the same way people do. Knowledge graphs provide the connective tissue that allows AI to scale reliably across procurement, finance, and supply chain domains. 📊

Training AI Responsibly—Without Compromising Trust 🔐

One of the most common concerns from customers is how enterprise data is used by AI. Ryan clarified that SAP’s approach centers on pre‑trained models that leverage customer data at inference time, not to train shared public models.

Looking ahead, however, the greatest value will come from customer‑specific AI—models fine‑tuned to an organization’s processes, terminology, and data. This mirrors how employees are trained internally and enables AI to function as a true digital colleague. Strong governance, security, and transparency will remain essential as this capability evolves. 🧩

A New Interaction Model for Enterprise Work 👓

As AI matures, the way users interact with enterprise systems will change. Static dashboards may give way to dynamically generated views. Mobile and tablet‑based approvals will become more common. Over time, augmented and mixed‑reality interfaces may further reduce friction by delivering insights directly in context.

The unifying theme is abstraction: AI handles orchestration, data retrieval, and execution, while users focus on judgment, strategy, and outcomes. This shift has the potential to significantly reshape how procurement teams operate at scale. 🌐

The Future of Procurement Is Human‑Led, AI‑Powered 🎯

AI will not replace procurement professionals—but it will redefine their role. The future belongs to organizations that invest early in data foundations, AI literacy, and trust‑based adoption. As AI evolves from tool, to copilot, to agent, SAP’s focus remains clear: embed intelligence responsibly, keep humans in control, and deliver tangible business value.

The question is no longer whether AI will transform procurement—but how intentionally leaders choose to shape that transformation. 🔮

LISTEN HERE:

Apple:  https://podcasts.apple.com/us/podcast/episode-029-diving-deep-into-ai-in-procurement-with/id1764078646?i=1000760634770

Spotify:  Spotify:  https://open.spotify.com/episode/0DoASvDgt3tzox6D6KAmu6?si=QXxta4KzS6mO7YF2SS9MVQ

entry.title: SAP Ariba Procure to Order 2605 Release Key Innovations
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Community, and welcome to our 2605 Key Innovations blog.

The upcoming SAP Ariba P2O features introduce updates across key areas of the buying experience, including Network Catalogs, Guided Buying, and buying through the mobile app.

These changes are aimed at making it easier for users to find what they need, follow the right buying paths, and place orders wherever they are. This blog highlights what’s coming and what you may want to be aware of ahead of the release.

SAP Business Network Catalog Integration with Guided Buying (GUIDEB‑1337)

This feature integrates the SAP Business Network catalog directly into SAP Ariba Buying, allowing Guided Buying users to search and select items from SAP Business Network supplier catalogs alongside their existing private catalogs.

With this integration, users can discover new products and suppliers they may not have previously been aware of, increasing the range of available options and improving overall buying choice. By adopting the SAP Business Network catalog.

Key benefits for buyers and suppliers include:

  • Access to SAP Business Network catalog items alongside existing private catalog content
  • Administrative control over whether users can access the SAP Business Network catalog
  • Support for supplier diversity and innovation through broader supplier visibility
  • Increased exposure for suppliers to a wider buyer base, driving engagement and transaction potential

 To utilise this feature, the following parameter must be enabled:

PARAM_ENABLE_NEXTGEN_SBN_CATALOG

 

Guided Buying Forms Enhancements:

This release includes two small but useful enhancements to the Guided Buying forms capability. Both changes are designed to support form designers while improving usability for business users.

PowerPoint File Attachment Support for Forms (FRM-652)

Forms now support PowerPoint file attachments in the Attachment widget. This enhancement allows form users to upload and submit PPT and PPTX files, expanding the range of supported document formats. Previously, attaching PowerPoint files was not possible, which limited document‑sharing options in certain business scenarios.

 

 *This feature will be automatically enabled

Enhanced Column Display Control in Data Table Capability (FRM-751)

Business users can now control which columns are displayed in data tables within forms. A new ToolPanel property has been introduced for the Data Table widget. When enabled during form design, this adds a Columns panel to the data table in the form document.

Using this panel, business users can expand Pivot Mode, review the list of available columns, and hide or show columns as needed, making large or complex data tables easier to work with.

User interface changes

  • A new ToolPanel slider is available in the Properties tab of the Data Table widget.

*This feature will be automatically enabled

 

SAP Ariba Procurement mobile app:

We’ve heard your feedback on mobile buying and approvals in SAP Ariba, and this release introduces enhancements designed to make working on the go more flexible and efficient. The following updates focus on improving approval capabilities and expanding what users can do directly from the SAP Ariba Procurement mobile app.

Approving Contract Requests in the SAP Ariba Procurement Mobile App (BMAA-508)

Users can now approve contract requests directly from the SAP Ariba Procurement mobile app. Previously, approving contract requests required signing in to the web application, which was not always convenient for approvers who are frequently mobile.

With this enhancement, approvers can:

  • Review key contract request details
  • Approve or deny requests
  • Add comments directly within the mobile app

A new Contract Requests tile has also been added to the Your To‑Dos section of the app. This tile displays the number of pending contract requests, and tapping it opens a dedicated screen showing all requests awaiting approval.

To use this feature, the following parameter must be enabled:

Application.Mobile.EnableUsersToApproveContractRequests

 

Support for Creating Requisitions in 2NV and 3EN Integration Scenarios Using the SAP Ariba Procurement Mobile App (BMAA-510)

This release also expands mobile support for requisition creation in 2NV and 3EN integration scenarios. Users can now create requisitions directly in the SAP Ariba Procurement mobile app using both catalog and non‑catalog material items.

Once a purchase requisition is submitted via the mobile app, it is replicated in the SAP S/4HANA system.

Additional capabilities include:

  • Buyers can add comments and attachments at the line‑item level
  • Approvers can review, approve, or reject purchase requisitions directly from the mobile app

To utilise this feature, the following parameter must be enabled:

Application.Mobile.EnableUsersToApproveContractRequests

 

Overall, this release continues to focus on making buying simpler, more flexible, and more efficient—whether through improved Guided Buying capabilities, expanded catalog access, or enhanced mobile functionality. Thanks for reading!

Resources:

  • Feature related materials:
    • SAP Ariba Connect - Release Readiness (Customers except CIM)
    • Readiness Community page (For CIM customers)
  • SAP Community for Intelligent Spend Management page
  • SAP Help Portal pages - Procurement and Network
  • What’s New

entry.title: SAP Ariba 2605 Release Highlights
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Community! 

We're eager to share the latest feature release highlights across the SAP Ariba Suite. 

Below, you'll find product area blogs outlining (current-gen) features being released in May 2026. 

SAP Ariba Category Management 2605 Release Highlights
