entry.title: SAP Ariba Invoicing 2605 Release Key Innovations
entry.content:
Hello SAP Ariba Community!  We’re excited to share the latest enhancements in SAP Ariba Invoicing (formally SAP Ariba Central Invoice Management (CIM)) coming in the Q2 2026 release! As part of our ongoing commitment to innovate and streamline invoice management, we’re happy to announce these key enhancements which are designed to simplify the user experience and operations as well as deliver greater efficiencies overall to our customers.  We hope you enjoy these highlights!

1.  Support of Ad Hoc Approvers in the Approval Workflow (CIM-2578)

In Accounts Payable, exceptions are normal. An unexpected requirement such as an urgent quarter-end invoice can require extra eyes. Until now, that often meant stopping and reconfiguring an approval workflow or sending an invoice back to the start.

In this release, we’re introducing an important capability that many of our users have been asking for: the ability to add or remove approvers in SAP Ariba Invoicing to help your teams handle exceptions without slowing down the process. It’s a simple feature that has a big impact on continuity, cycle times, and on the overall user experience.

Support of Ad Hoc Approvers in the Approval Workflow:

  • You can add and remove ad hoc approval steps while a supplier invoice is already in the approval process.
  • Previously, approvers could only be configured before the workflow started in Workflows for Supplier Invoice Approval.
  • You can dynamically add or remove up to 10 additional approval steps during the approval process to address real-time needs and keep work moving.

Invoice approvals rarely follow a perfect script. This enhancement gives AP, procurement, and finance the flexibility to adapt to unexpected situations while maintaining control.

Key business benefits include:

  • Faster cycle times: Handle exceptions in-flight instead of restarting or reconfiguring the workflow. This keeps critical invoices moving and reduces end-of-period bottlenecks.
  • Better control and compliance: Insert the right approvers at the right time for sensitive categories, high-value invoices, or regulatory checks—without bypassing governance.
  • Less rework: Avoid sending invoices back to drafting or re-submitting just to change approvers. Reduce back-and-forth between AP and requesters.
  • Improved collaboration: Bring in subject matter experts or temporary approvers when needed, then remove those steps once the exception is resolved.    

Below is an example to visualize this enhancement:         

2.  Support of Approvers Based on Spend Limits (CIM-3050)

With this feature, you can now add additional approvers to a supplier invoice based on user approval limits. It allows you to automatically escalate supplier invoices to approvers in the approval hierarchy when the invoice gross amount exceeds an approver's defined limit.

How it works at a glance:

  • You define approval limits for users or roles and maintain your approval hierarchy in SAP Ariba Invoicing.
  • When the invoice gross amount exceeds the current approver’s limit, the system automatically adds the next approver(s) in the hierarchy until the right authorization level is reached.
  • The feature augments your existing approval rules to improve coverage without adding complexity for end users.

This feature introduces important benefits and controls:

  • Stronger financial control: Enforce approval policies consistently by making sure invoices are always reviewed by approvers with the appropriate spending authority.
  • Faster cycle times: Reduce back-and-forth and rerouting caused by invoices landing with approvers who don’t have sufficient limits.
  • Improved compliance and auditability: Create a clear, auditable trail that shows why and how an invoice moved up the chain.
  • Scalability as you grow: As organizations change, limits and hierarchies can be updated without reworking every approval flow.

In the screenshot, you can see the new Approvers based on the spend limit field:

3.  Enhancement to Invoice Rejection Reason (CIM- 3439)

With this release, we’ve introduced clearer supplier notifications for invoice rejections in SAP Ariba Invoicing.  Exception handling is part of every accounts payable operation. When you need to reject an invoice, it should be clear who gets notified and easy to prove that the notification went out. With this release, we have made this simpler and more transparent.

What’s new:

  • Enhancement to Invoice Rejection Reason: When you click Reject Invoice, the system now shows the email ID (email address) of the supplier contact who will receive the rejection notification.
  • Automatic confirmation in Comments: After you reject, the invoice’s Comments tab records a system comment that confirms the notification was sent to the supplier, including the supplier’s email ID.

Some key benefits include:

  • Greater transparency: AP analysts see exactly who will be notified before they confirm a rejection.
  • Faster dispute resolution: Clear routing reduces back-and-forth and helps suppliers get the right information the first time.
  • Better accountability: A time-stamped comment creates an audit trail without manual notes.
  • Improved supplier experience: Suppliers get a timely, directed notice and can act faster.

The screenshot below displays the Supplier Email that will be notified of the invoice rejection:

These enhancements are intended to demonstrate SAP's commitment to building solutions that support and enable your business process needs.

Stay tuned for more enhancements in SAP Ariba Invoicing to drive efficiency and flexibility in your business process.

entry.title: What's New in 2605, Release Navigator for Spend Management
entry.content:
Q2 2026 is here and with a new quarter, comes new innovation! The 2605 updates for the release navigator are live; this update is primarily focused on content maintenance. 

SAP Ariba Procurement and SAP Business Network Quarterly Releases are scheduled for the weekend May 15th, 2026. Make sure to go to the Release Navigator for Spend Management to find the most recent Webcasts, Feature Lists, Tutorial Videos, Blogs, and more!

Tool Overview

The Release Navigator for Spend Management is a tool provided by SAP Enterprise Support designed to be your central hub for understanding and managing new software releases for SAP Ariba, SAP Business Network, SAP Fieldglass, & SAP Concur. It allows you to easily locate essential resources for upcoming releases and quickly find cross-solution materials, documentation, webcasts, and more all in one tool!

2605 Feature Release Highlights for SAP Ariba & Business Network:

  • Enhancements to the SAP Integration Suite, Managed Gateway for Spend Management and SAP Business Network Add-On [NGPROCINT-452]
  • UI Updates Across SAP Ariba Category Management Apps [ARBCATA-607]
  • Additional Authorizations for AI Copilot Joule Scenarios in Supplier Management [SLP-2412]

Don’t forget to register for the 2605 Release Webcasts here.

Innovation Schedule

To ensure a smooth user experience, the Release Navigator introduces new user‑experience updates once per year in August, alongside refreshed release content.

The Release Navigator will update automatically over the coming weeks—no action is required on your end.

entry.title: SAP AI: Beyond the Hype and Into the Real World
entry.content:
Three days in Excel hell. Twenty supplier bids. One AI agent that knows your data. This is the SAP AI story your vendor deck won't tell you — first in a series.

I've been on the digital transformation journey for more years than I care to admit. I truly believe that constant evolution is the only way to stay relevant in procurement, but lately, it feels like the word "transformation" has been completely hijacked by a single two-letter acronym: AI. Everywhere I turn, AI is the only topic in the room. It is being pitched as the ultimate cure-all, yet for most of us, the daily reality still involves wrestling with legacy systems and messy data.

After endless conversations about AI's impact today, where it is going, and how it fits into our daily lives, I've realized that what we need is not more noise, but a clear roadmap.

My intention with this series is to provide a solid understanding of where we are today, set some realistic expectations, and point out where the magic really happens when we get it right.

We will look at what SAP is doing broadly with Business AI, while zooming in specifically on the procurement space where the impact is most immediate. Each post in this series will focus on a specific piece of the puzzle or a real-world scenario, helping you not only navigate this AI transformation but ensuring you actually see tangible value at the end of it. And I promise: no marketing fluff and no buzzword bingo.

 

The Anatomy of Success: How the Pieces Actually Fit Together

Stop thinking about AI as a single feature you can switch on. It's a high-performance system built from three distinct, non-negotiable layers. Pull any one out and the whole thing fails to deliver value. Here's the blueprint:

 The three layers of the SAP AI stack. Remove any one and the system fails to deliver value.

Each layer has a clear job. BTP is where your code actually runs and your security lives. SAP Business Data Cloud is the part that prevents your AI from getting confused — it makes sure a "Supplier" in one system is the same thing as a "Vendor" in another. Joule is the conversational front door that takes your natural-language request and orchestrates the work across every back-end system. Everything else in this post depends on these three working together.

 

Four Flavors of SAP AI You'll Actually Encounter

Not all AI is created equal — and you'll meet at least four different flavors in any real SAP deployment. Each one solves a different problem and shows up in your workflow in a different way. Here's how to recognize them:

 Four flavors of SAP AI. Each one solves a different problem — and you'll meet all of them

 

Talk Is Cheap. Let's Look at a Real Scenario.

We've all heard the lofty promises of AI, but talk is notoriously cheap. Instead of another high-level theory, I want to show you a practical scenario that has already been built and is currently saving procurement experts hundreds of hours. This is not some futuristic concept waiting for a release date — it's how we're solving real-world complexity right now.

 The Bid Analysis Agent in action — what 80% of the heavy lifting looks like.

WHAT HAPPENS TODAY WITHOUT AI

You're stuck in Excel hell for three days. You're manually pivoting data, trying to run "what if" scenarios, and likely making a few coffee-fueled typos along the way. By the time you finally have an answer, the market data has already shifted and your analysis is effectively a historical document.

THE BASIC AI EXPERIENCE

You try to use a generic, public AI tool. You upload a spreadsheet and ask for an analysis. It gives you a shallow "cheapest is best" answer, hallucinates a few shipping routes that don't exist, and potentially leaks your sensitive pricing data to the public web. Fast, but dangerous — and completely lacking business context.

THE REALISTIC SAP AI FUTURE

You use a Bid Analysis Agent grounded in your actual Ariba data. You ask Joule to show the award scenario that minimizes risk if fuel prices spike. The agent evaluates the trade-offs, highlights bid outliers, and suggests a data-driven strategy in seconds by factoring in unit prices, shipping, and risk data. You're still the decision-maker — but the AI has handled 80% of the analytical heavy lifting for you.


Straight Talk: Paying the Data Tax

AI in 2026 is not about replacing the procurement professional. It is about capacity. It's about getting that 20% to 30% of your week back from repetitive, manual work.

But let's be real about the Data Tax. I'm not suggesting every organization is starting from scratch. In fact, most customers I talk to have done a great job with their supplier and master data. The real mess usually hides elsewhere: in the offline spreadsheets, the unstructured sourcing history, and the fragmented procurement processes that haven't quite made it into the system yet.

 

Two data realities. One of them turns your AI into a confident liar.

Good data is structured, centralized, and consistent. It means having one version of the truth, so that when you ask an AI for a risk assessment, it isn't hallucinating because it found three different versions of the same contract.

Bad data is the fragmented manual sprawl — the tribal knowledge and the manual workarounds that live outside your digital core.

When you try to use AI with bad data, the implications are simple: the AI becomes "confidently wrong." It will give you an answer, but that answer will be based on a fragment of reality. Paying the Data Tax is not a one-time activity or a weekend cleanup. It's an ongoing evolution in how you store and leverage your information — a commitment to continuous improvement where your AI outputs steadily improve as your data strategy matures.

You simply cannot ignore the data layer of the architecture and expect the interaction layer to give you accurate results.

What's Next

Moving forward, this series will dive deep into the specific AI scenarios and features that drive actual savings in both time and money. I'll be sharing not just what we're building at SAP, but also the real-world best practices I'm seeing from other leading organizations that are successfully closing the "execution gap." There's a wealth of peer knowledge out there that can benefit us all.

I have specific use cases planned, but I'm always interested in your perspective. If there's a particular challenge you're facing or a piece of the puzzle you want to see solved, let me know what you'd like me to cover in future chapters.

entry.title: EDI in the age of agentic AI, the structural mismatch nobody is naming
entry.content:
Somewhere in most people’s houses, there is a road atlas. Beautifully printed, comprehensive, the place you were looking for always on the join between two pages or on the fold where the binding had eaten the postcode. Then the internet arrived and you printed out a route from a website, which was a digital map in name and a dead letter on the passenger seat. The thing that actually changed driving was location services. The moment the map knew where you were and where the closures were, it stopped being an artefact and became a network you could query. Then Waze went further: routing you using information contributed by every other driver on the road. A car on the hard shoulder. A speed camera. A tailback the official feed would not catch for twenty minutes. The map was no longer a thing you read. It was a network where every participant made the whole more intelligent for everyone else.

So, time to drag this back to my actual day job, which is business networks and S/4HANA. Every S/4 programme has to answer a deceptively simple question: how will this enterprise communicate with the outside world once the transformation is done? The old plumbing breaks. IDocs are restructured or retired, partner mappings need re-testing, and on-premises middleware that has quietly carried the business for fifteen years, invisible until the moment it failed, cannot keep up. The bill comes due. But the real choice is not how to fix the plumbing. It is whether the connectivity layer should remain a way of moving documents between two known parties, or become a queryable network that an autonomous agent can reason over. The answer the market is offering, namely modernise the EDI, move it to the cloud, add a bit of AI, is the corporate equivalent of printing out a route from the website. Technically more modern. Functionally the same piece of paper.

Give cloud EDI its due first. Moving an on-premises EDI estate into a managed cloud genuinely helps: better resilience, faster partner onboarding, centralised monitoring, and no more single-point-of-failure when your one EDI specialist retires. I am not arguing against any of it. I am arguing that none of it changes what the architecture is.

Cloud EDI is still EDI. It is still bilateral. It is still static. It produces a dead document, accurate at the moment of transmission, useless the moment anything changes, and it has nothing useful to offer an autonomous agent. Agents do not just process documents after the fact. They need to interrogate live, networked data, who has capacity now, at what price, under what constraints, and EDI has no way of exposing that.

This is the part that needs spelling out. Consider a worked example. A buyer agent at a Tier 1 manufacturer receives an order for three thousand units of a sub-assembly. To place that order well, the agent needs to know which suppliers across the network have capacity, at what price, with what lead time, tariff exposure, and carbon intensity. It needs to consider whether splitting the order across two suppliers reduces risk enough to justify the higher unit price, and whether either supplier is constrained by a Tier 2 component that has gone short this week. I have sat through several of those demos this year. Not one of them could have been accomplished over a one-to-one document pipe. Not one. The agent needs a network where it can ask a question and get an answer that reflects the current state of that network.

The architectural requirement is the same whatever you call it: networked, queryable, shared state across thousands of partners, with event-level visibility and the means to act. SAP Business Network is the specific instantiation of this within the S/4HANA ecosystem. The label matters less than the property. EDI was engineered in the 1970s to do the opposite of all of it.

Watch the market on this one. Read any vendor argument for "modernising your EDI to the cloud" carefully and you will find them describing a system that decouples mapping from the ERP, holds partner profiles centrally, and offers a real-time cockpit across thousands of partners. That is not modernised EDI. That is a network with a different name on the door. The market is quietly building the right thing while loudly selling the wrong word for it.

Two numbers tell you the shape of it. Interos, in its 6 Supply Chain Risks for 2026 report, finds that 90% of organisations remain blind to sub-tier disruptions for up to 48 hours. APQC's 2026 Supply Chain Priorities and Challenges: Cross-Industry Report finds that only one in three organisations have a strategy this year to increase visibility into lower-tier suppliers. Two-thirds are not even planning to do anything about it. One-to-one connections cannot reach those lower tiers. They cannot reach an agentic workflow either.

You already know I work in this space. The companies already on a network are building compound advantage in supplier visibility, agentic readiness, and network intelligence that a one-to-one pipe cannot replicate after the fact.

This should not be a Phase 2 conversation. It should not be kicked to 2028. It should not be left for the next team to discover. By then the programme team has moved on. The decision is business as usual. Nobody around the table remembers it was once a choice. A one-to-one pipe cannot reroute when a supplier fails, recompute when a tariff lands, or answer when an agent asks it a question. A network can do all three. The companies that defer will spend the next three years falling behind quietly, and in an agentic world three years is no longer a planning horizon. It is a generation. Kicking this to 2028 is not a deferral. It is a decision to fall behind, taken by people who will not be there to see it land.

The road atlas works. So does the printed page from the route planner. Neither of them is going to tell you about the lorry on the hard shoulder at the next junction, or that a faster route has just opened up that the rest of your competitors are already taking.

entry.title: 4 Ways CPOs Can Drive Procurement Outcomes with AI
entry.content:
The CPOs I've been talking to are navigating a different kind of pressure than they faced two years ago. It's not just about cost discipline anymore. Tariff volatility is forcing them to review supplier contracts. Over-concentrated supply bases can be exposed overnight by a single geopolitical event. And boards expect measurable AI ROI in weeks, not months. Procurement is being asked to do more with less and to manage more risk with greater precision.

There's no shortage of AI tools promising to help here — but the problem is that most AI in procurement is being deployed as a stand-alone agent or as an automated workflow that operates without visibility into what's happening across the rest of the business.

When sourcing isn't aware of spend insights or supplier-risk signals, you don't get AI-driven procurement, you get AI-assisted silos.

You need Autonomous Spend Management with SAP, which is a connected system where:

  • AI works across the entire source-to-pay process 
  • A supplier risk surfaces immediately in sourcing
  • Compliance requirements are enforced automatically at the point of purchase
  • A category strategy informs every downstream decision.

Autonomous Spend solutions support faster decisions, fewer handoffs, and consistent outcomes at scale — proactively identifying supply risks, optimizing supplier relationships, localizing compliance for invoices and expenses, and uncovering untapped savings opportunities.

What I'm Hearing from CPOs

Over the past few months, I've had the privilege of speaking with procurement leaders across industries and geographies at SAP Sapphire in Orlando and at the Procurement Leaders AI Forum in London, and in a lot of conversations in between. What struck me wasn't any single insight, but rather how consistent the pressure is, regardless of company size or sector.

In Orlando, I spoke to a CPO from a global manufacturer who had just spent three months renegotiating supplier contracts because of tariff changes he hadn't seen coming. A few weeks later in London, I sat down with a procurement transformation lead at a financial institution who was dealing with a completely different set of facts but describing the exact same feeling. The same sense that the ground keeps shifting.

This isn't a moment of isolated disruption. It's a structural shift in what procurement leadership requires.

What This Means for the Function

The questions I’m hearing aren’t 'which tasks can AI handle?' They're 'what does my function become when AI handles these tasks?' That's a much more consequential question.

Most procurement teams today spend the majority of their capacity on coordination: routing approvals, researching suppliers, answering questions although answers already exist somewhere in their systems, and manually connecting information that should already be connected.

When AI begins to execute these routine tasks autonomously — routing, researching, recommending, and enforcing policy — procurement capacity shifts. Your team starts to be seen as strategists. That shift compounds over time: lower cycle times, fewer leakage points, and a function that can respond to a tariff change or a supply disruption in hours rather than weeks.

The four examples below show what this looks like in practice when a procurement organization navigates a major strategic shift. Individually, each story is about a person using AI to work differently. Together, they describe what an elevated function actually looks like.

Kate: AI-Powered Category Management

Kate had her procurement world figured out. She’d spent five years perfecting steel and electronics categories, with color-coded spreadsheets she could navigate in her sleep.

Then her company decided to pivot to electric vehicles and her carefully organized category plans became obsolete overnight.

She needed to quickly build new market intelligence, identify qualified suppliers, and create category frameworks that could adapt as technology evolved.

The traditional category development process wouldn't work while the market was moving this fast.

Fortunately, Kate had Category Management Agents to help her transform her approach. Within days she had access to:

  • Comprehensive 360-degree supplier profiles with continuous updates
  • Strategy recommendations grounded in proven frameworks
  • Real-time risk highlights and market intelligence

Instead of spending weeks researching new components, Kate used AI-driven strategy recommendations to build a new category with intelligent subcategories. She had a complete view of market spend and supplier dynamics in one place, alongside recommendations with risk highlights that helped her make informed decisions.

Expected impact: Reduced maverick spend by 5-15% by aligning demand to category strategy and approved channels.* 

Marcus: Intelligent Sourcing at Scale

Identifying the right strategic category was just the beginning. The pressure had now shifted to Marcus, the sourcing manager.

Marcus knew the drill: RFPs meant late nights, multiple spreadsheets, and that sinking feeling when he realized he'd been researching the wrong supplier for three hours.

Without AI support, it could take him weeks to pull together detailed tech specs, quality certifications, testing protocols, and environmental impact criteria. He was conducting manual research on potential suppliers across fragmented data sources — checking one system for financial stability, another for past performance, and yet another for current capabilities.

With Sourcing Assistant, Marcus started a conversation with Joule and kicked off his RFP with just a few prompts. Joule analyzed his requirements and business objectives, articulated what he needed to source, and eliminated the research and guesswork, delivering:

  • Supplier recommendations based on his unique business environment
  • Draft comprehensive questionnaires
  • Transparent bid analysis with AI-generated counteroffers

Using data already in SAP like past contracts, supplier history, and performance records, Joule made recommendations that were specific to Marcus's business context. 

Expected impact: Improved total cost of ownership by 5-15% through better specifications, supplier selection, and negotiation support.*

Christopher: Streamlined Buying Decisions

With a strong category strategy and sourcing plan in place, value could still leak out — and it often does at the buying stage. Christopher from operations ran into exactly this problem when his team ran low on high-voltage cables for a critical production line.

Without AI guidance, he faced analysis paralysis. Should he order cables with additional wiring harnesses? Which supplier offered the fastest delivery for his specific requirements? Meanwhile the approval process became a bottleneck, bouncing between Finance for budget verification and Procurement for contract compliance.

The Requisition Assistant transformed Christopher's buying experience by:

  • Guiding him automatically to compliant choices
  • Streamlining approvals with pre-populated context
  • Enforcing policy compliance and preventing maverick spending

Instead of getting stuck in technical decisions and approval delays, AI agents guided Christopher to compliant choices by suggesting cables that matched his specs with approved suppliers while routing his requests to the right approvers.

Expected impact: Saved 2-4% through channel consolidation and higher on-contract compliance.* 

Priya: Services Procurement Made Simple

But the complexity didn't end with direct materials. Priya from facilities management needed to secure installation services for the new cables — and procuring services introduced an entirely different set of challenges. She had to draft a comprehensive Statement of Work (SOW) but lacked the expertise to define the requirements clearly and wasn't familiar with the approval workflows.

The Services Procurement Assistant became Priya's expert guide, offering:

  • A simple conversational interface with Joule
  • Comprehensive, AI-generated SOW drafts
  • Automatic routing with proper documentation

She simply told Joule what she needed: installation services for the London facility. Joule confirmed understanding, drafted an SOW with clear terms, milestones, and performance metrics, and automatically routed the request.

Expected impact: Reduced compliance breaches by up to 91% with policy-by-design workflows and standardized SOWs.* 

The procurement function is at an inflection point.

Connected AI enforces policy by design, reducing exceptions without slowing the business. Organizations that connect AI across the entire source-to-pay process are building an advantage that compounds faster responses to supply disruption and greater team capacity for strategic decisions. Organizations that deploy AI in isolated pockets will capture limited value. And the gap between those two outcomes is widening every quarter.

If you want to see what this looks like in practice for your organization, let's talk.

  • Already using SAP Ariba? Contact your Account Executive to learn how you can activate Joule.
  • New to SAP Ariba? Reach out to our team to request a demo.

*Source:  BCG/SAP Study (2026) on agentic AI in spend management. Values based on a net-new €10B company with €5B procurement spend. Value unlock tiers: XL: above €500M, L: €200M–€500M, M: €50M–€200M, S: below €50M. Actual results vary by baseline, category mix, and policy maturity.

entry.title: SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass Earn 2026 TrustRadius Top Rated and Buyer's Choice Awards
entry.content:
We're proud to share that SAP Ariba and SAP Fieldglass have each earned two prestigious 2026 TrustRadius awards, recognition that means a great deal because it comes entirely from the people who matter most: our customers.

 

 SAP Ariba has been named a 2026 TrustRadius Top Rated and 2026 Buyer's Choice winner in the Procurement category, while SAP Fieldglass has received the same honors in the Vendor Management category.

 

 

What These Awards Mean

TrustRadius awards are unlike many industry accolades. The Top Rated award is not a participation award, but rather it is hard-earned and based entirely on active customer sentiment, with no analyst input and no preference for TrustRadius customers. The Buyer's Choice award goes even further, signifying that a product goes above and beyond in meeting expectations across capabilities, value, and customer relationships.

In short: these awards aren't given — they're earned, review by review, from verified users sharing their real experiences.

Recognized by the People Who Use These Products Every Day

For SAP Ariba, customers consistently highlight its ability to streamline end-to-end procurement, from sourcing and contracting to supplier management and compliance. For SAP Fieldglass, users point to its strength in managing the full contingent workforce lifecycle, from onboarding external workers to time tracking, statements of work, and payment.

Earning recognition in both the Top Rated and Buyer's Choice categories across Procurement and Vendor Management reflects SAP's ongoing commitment to delivering Spend Management solutions that drive real business value, not just on paper, but in practice, for the organizations relying on these tools every day.

Thank You to Our Customers

These awards belong to the customers who took the time to share their honest feedback on TrustRadius. Your voices shape how the market understands these products, and they inspire us to keep improving.

If you'd like to explore what customers are saying about SAP Ariba or SAP Fieldglass, visit their profiles on TrustRadius and read the verified reviews firsthand.
